Curriculum → High-level design interview prep → Application architecture and APIs → Real-time delivery: WebSockets, SSE, and long polling

Lesson 5 of 5

Real-time delivery: WebSockets, SSE, and long polling

When users expect live updates, choose a delivery model that matches fan-out shape, connection count, and the direction of data flow.

25-35 min HLD flow Application architecture and APIs 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Real-time features are common in interviews because they force you to reason about connection state, pub/sub fan-out, and degraded modes.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Pick by communication pattern Chat and collaborative editing often need WebSockets2. Handle fan-out and connection state Use pub/sub or streams to broadcast updates across nodes3. Degraded modes matter Keep reconnect logic idempotent

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Real-time features are common in interviews because they force you to reason about connection state, pub/sub fan-out, and degraded modes. 2. Pick by communication pattern Chat and collaborative editing often need WebSockets3. Handle fan-out and connection state Use pub/sub or streams to broadcast updates across nodes4. Degraded modes matter Keep reconnect logic idempotent
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Real-time features are common in interviews because they force you to reason about connection state, pub/sub fan-out, and degraded modes.

What to say first

  • Choose a protocol based on data direction and scale.
  • Discuss connection management and pub/sub fan-out.
  • Plan a fallback mode when sockets fail.
2

Study section

Pick by communication pattern

WebSockets support bidirectional low-latency streams; Server-Sent Events fit server-to-client updates; long polling is simpler but less efficient at scale.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Chat and collaborative editing often need WebSockets
  • Live dashboards often fit SSE
  • Long polling remains useful for simpler infrastructure or legacy environments
3

Study section

Handle fan-out and connection state

Persistent connections move cost from request rate to connection management, heartbeat handling, and per-node state.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Use pub/sub or streams to broadcast updates across nodes
  • Connection sharding and sticky routing may be necessary
  • Back pressure and slow-consumer handling are critical
4

Study section

Degraded modes matter

If real-time delivery fails, many products can fall back to periodic refresh or delayed notifications. Say that explicitly.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Keep reconnect logic idempotent
  • Replay missed events from a durable log when correctness matters
  • Track connection counts, reconnect rate, and lag

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Choose a protocol based on data direction and scale.
  • Discuss connection management and pub/sub fan-out.
  • Plan a fallback mode when sockets fail.
  • Observe lag, reconnects, and slow consumers.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Using WebSockets everywhere by default.
  • Ignoring sticky routing or shared subscription state.
  • Forgetting replay or gap filling when updates matter.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • How would you deliver live order-status updates?
  • Why might SSE be better than WebSockets for a dashboard?
  • How do you recover missed events after a reconnect?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Queues, streams, and background work Caching, asynchrony, and resilienceFeeds and timelines Common product design patterns

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: Real-time delivery: WebSockets, SSE, and long polling

Compare synchronous service hops against queued fan-out so you can explain where coupling shows up in production. Apply it to real-time delivery: websockets, sse, and long polling so the trade-offs feel concrete.

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Explain the concept

Teach "Real-time delivery: WebSockets, SSE, and long polling" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Guardrails

  • How would you deliver live order-status updates?
  • Pick by communication pattern
  • Handle fan-out and connection state
  • Choose a protocol based on data direction and scale.

Suggested Structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model
